items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology of Poisonings in the U.S.
items.content: The epidemiology of poison exposures reveals that most cases are accidental, involve children under 6 years old, and occur at home. Pharmaceuticals are a significant contributor to serious or fatal poisonings.
keyPoints: More than 5 million poison exposures occur annually in the U.S.
Most exposures are acute, unintentional, single-agent incidents occurring at home (>90%)
Pharmaceuticals involved in 47% of exposures and 84% of serious or fatal cases
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · Poisoning and Drug Overdose | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Trends and Impact of Drug-Related Fatalities
items.content: Fatal drug overdoses have been rising since 1999, with synthetic opioids like fentanyl being major contributors. The CDC reported over 106,600 deaths in 2021 due to drug overdose.
keyPoints: Drug-related fatalities increased steadily since 1999
Over 106,600 deaths attributed to drug overdose in 2021
Age-adjusted overdose death rate was 32.4 per 100,000 in 2021
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · Poisoning and Drug Overdose | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Hospitalization and Mortality Rates from Poisonings
items.content: A small percentage of poison exposures require hospitalization, but intentional overdoses have a higher mortality rate. Acetaminophen is frequently implicated in fatal cases.
keyPoints: 20–25% of exposures need health-professional evaluation; 5% require hospitalization
<1% overall mortality from poisoning exposures
Mortality rate is 1–2% among hospitalized patients with intentional overdose or drug complications
Acetaminophen most often implicated in fatal poisonings
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · Poisoning and Drug Overdose | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Importance of Comprehensive History in Poisoning Diagnosis
items.content: A thorough history is crucial for diagnosing poisoning as it can mimic other illnesses. Key elements include the time, route, and circumstances of exposure; details about drugs or chemicals involved; symptom onset and severity; first-aid measures taken; medical and psychiatric background; and occupational information.
keyPoints: Include time, route, duration, and circumstances of exposure in history
Gather information on the name and amount of each drug or chemical involved
Consider patient confusion or unwillingness to admit exposure
Look for suspicious circumstances such as sudden illness or psychiatric issues
Consult family, friends, paramedics, police, pharmacists, physicians, and employers
Ask explicitly about prescribed, over-the-counter, herbal medications, and recreational drug use
Review social media posts for self-harm plans involving drugs
Consider all medications in the patient's home or belongings if history is unclear
Use state prescription monitoring program (PMP) to check controlled substance use
Identify pills and chemicals using imprint codes and labels with reference texts or poison centers
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · DIAGNOSIS | HISTORY

items.title: Recognizing Red Flags in Poisoning Cases
items.content: Certain red flags should prompt suspicion of poisoning, such as unexplained sudden illness in healthy individuals, psychiatric history, recent life changes, or exposure to chemicals. Special attention is needed for patients arriving from abroad or after arrest due to potential 'body packing' or 'body stuffing'. Occupational exposures and unfamiliar poisons may require consultation with toxicologists or poison centers.
keyPoints: Suspicious circumstances include sudden illness in healthy individuals
Consider psychiatric history, especially depression or bipolar disorder
Recent changes in health, economic status, or social relationships are red flags
Onset of illness during chemical work or after ingestion should raise suspicion
Suspect 'body packing' or 'body stuffing' with recent travel or arrest
Consult family, friends, and authorities for additional information
Review safety data sheets (SDS) for occupational exposures
Unfamiliar poisons may need identification via toxicologists or poison centers
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · DIAGNOSIS | HISTORY

items.title: Importance of Vital Signs and Neurologic Examination in Poisoning
items.content: The initial physical examination for suspected poisoning should focus on vital signs, cardiopulmonary system, and neurologic status. This includes assessing neuromuscular abnormalities like dyskinesia, dystonia, fasciculations, myoclonus, rigidity, and tremors. Focal neurologic findings are rare in poisoning but may indicate a structural CNS lesion.
keyPoints: Assess vital signs: pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, pulse oximetry, temperature, and neurologic status.
Neuromuscular abnormalities include dyskinesia, dystonia, fasciculations, myoclonus, rigidity, and tremors.
Focal neurologic findings suggest evaluation for structural CNS lesions.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Value of Physical Examination in Poisoning
items.content: Examination of the eyes, abdomen, bladder, skin, and orifices can reveal diagnostic clues. Eye examination includes checking for nystagmus and pupil size/reactivity. Abdominal assessment involves bowel activity, while bladder size is noted. Skin examination looks for burns, bullae, color changes, warmth, moisture, pressure sores, and puncture marks.
keyPoints: Examine eyes for nystagmus and pupil size/reactivity.
Assess abdomen for bowel activity and bladder size.
Inspect skin for burns, bullae, color changes, warmth, moisture, pressure sores, and puncture marks.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing the Stimulated Physiologic State in Poisoning
items.content: The stimulated physiologic state is characterized by increased pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, temperature, and neuromuscular activity. It can indicate sympathetic, anticholinergic, or hallucinogen poisoning, or drug withdrawal. Key features include mydriasis, hot dry skin, decreased bowel sounds, urinary retention, diaphoresis, pallor, increased bowel activity, nausea, vomiting, distress, and diarrhea.
keyPoints: Increased pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, temperature, and neuromuscular activity indicate stimulation.
Mydriasis is marked in anticholinergic poisoning; sympathetic poisoning shows some light reactivity.
Anticholinergic toxidrome: hot dry skin, decreased bowel sounds, urinary retention.
Sympathetic stimulation causes diaphoresis, pallor, increased bowel activity, nausea/vomiting/distress/diarrhea.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Depressed Physiologic State in Poisoning
items.content: Recognize the depressed physiologic state by observing decreased pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, temperature, and neuromuscular activity. This state is often caused by functional sympatholytics, cholinergic agents, opioids, or GABA-ergic agents. Miosis, especially pronounced in opioid and cholinergic poisoning, helps distinguish these cases from other depressant syndromes.
keyPoints: Decreased pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, temperature, and neuromuscular activity indicate a depressed physiologic state.
Causes include functional sympatholytics, cholinergic agents, opioids, and GABA-ergic agents.
Miosis is common in opioid and cholinergic poisoning.
Pronounced cardiovascular depression without significant CNS depression suggests direct or peripherally acting sympathetic involvement.
Significant vital-sign abnormalities occur at grade 3 or 4 physiologic depression.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 2)

items.title: Understanding the Discordant Physiologic State in Poisoning
items.content: The discordant physiologic state is characterized by mixed vital-sign and neuromuscular abnormalities. This can occur with asphyxiants, CNS syndromes, membrane-active agents, or AGMA inducers. Early pronounced changes suggest asphyxiant or membrane-active agent poisoning, while lack of such abnormalities suggests an AGMA inducer.
keyPoints: Mixed vital-sign and neuromuscular abnormalities indicate a discordant physiologic state.
Causes include asphyxiants, CNS syndromes, membrane-active agents, and AGMA inducers.
Simultaneous coma, seizures, hypotension, and tachyarrhythmias suggest membrane-active agent poisoning.
Normal vital signs with altered mental status may indicate an AGMA inducer.
Marked neuromuscular dysfunction without significant vital-sign abnormalities suggests a CNS syndrome.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Anion Gap in Poisoning
items.content: Increased anion gap metabolic acidosis is a key indicator of poisoning with methanol, ethylene glycol, and salicylates. It can also occur from any condition leading to hepatic, renal, or respiratory failure; seizures; or shock.
keyPoints: Increased AGMA common in advanced methanol, ethylene glycol, and salicylate intoxication
Serum lactate concentration low (< anion gap) in some poisonings, high (≈ anion gap) in others
Abnormally low anion gap can be due to elevated levels of bromide, calcium, iodine, lithium, or magnesium
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.title: Significance of Osmolal Gap in Toxicology
items.content: An increased osmolal gap (>10 mmol/L) suggests the presence of low-molecular-weight solutes, which can indicate specific types of poisoning.
keyPoints: Increased osmolal gap indicates low-molecular-weight solute presence
>10 mmol/L difference between measured and calculated serum osmolality is significant
Suggestive substances include acetone, alcohols (benzyl, ethanol, isopropanol, methanol), glycols, ethers, unmeasured cations or sugars
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.title: Role of ECG in Poisoning Diagnosis
items.content: ECG findings such as bradycardia, atrioventricular block, and QRS- or QT-interval prolongation can help diagnose specific poisonings.
keyPoints: Bradycardia and AV block may occur with $\alpha$-adrenergic agonists, beta blockers, etc.
QRS- and QT-interval prolongation linked to hyperkalemia, antidepressants, membrane-active drugs
Ventricular tachyarrhythmias seen in poisoning with cardiac glycosides, fluorides, etc.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.title: Role and Limitations of Rapid Qualitative Urine Drug Screens
items.content: Rapid qualitative urine drug screens serve as initial screening tools but cannot confirm the exact identity of substances. They are prone to false positives and negatives, limiting their diagnostic utility without confirmatory testing.
keyPoints: Screening tests should not be used for forensic purposes due to potential false results.
False positives can occur from pharmaceuticals like fluoroquinolones interfering with opiate screens.
Confirmatory testing (e.g., gas chromatography/mass spectrometry) is more accurate but time-consuming, often taking weeks.
Negative screening may indicate non-detectable substances or low concentrations at the time of sampling.
Qualitative drug screens are most valuable for severe or unexplained toxicities such as coma, seizures, and cardiovascular instability.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.title: Utility of Quantitative Serum Tests in Poisoning Management
items.content: Quantitative serum tests provide specific concentration levels that guide clinical management for various poisons, offering timely results essential for effective treatment.
keyPoints: Quantitative serum tests are useful for substances like acetaminophen, alcohols (ethylene glycol and methanol), anticonvulsants, barbiturates, digoxin, heavy metals, iron, lithium, salicylate, theophylline, carboxyhemoglobin, and methemoglobin.
Results from these tests are often available within an hour, aiding in prompt clinical decision-making.
These tests guide management by providing specific drug concentration levels.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Early Decontamination in Poisoning
items.content: Decontamination is most effective when performed soon after exposure and before the onset of symptoms. Establish IV access and initiate cardiac monitoring, especially for serious ingestions or unclear histories.
keyPoints: Decontamination should be prioritized during the pretoxic phase.
Establish IV access and cardiac monitoring early in management.
Assume maximal potential toxicity based on possible exposure.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.title: Management During the Toxic Phase of Poisoning
items.content: During the toxic phase, prioritize resuscitation and stabilization. Use clinical and laboratory findings to guide management. Consider IV glucose, naloxone, thiamine for altered mental status, coma, or seizures.
keyPoints: Resuscitation and stabilization are primary priorities.
IV line placement, oxygen saturation determination, cardiac monitoring, and continuous observation are essential.
Consider decontamination less likely to be effective during this phase.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.title: Enhancing Poison Elimination During Toxic Phase
items.content: Enhance poison elimination with measures like activated charcoal, urinary alkalinization, chelation therapy, and extracorporeal methods. Weigh risks against benefits and confirm diagnosis before proceeding.
keyPoints: Activated charcoal can enhance elimination of theophylline or carbamazepine.
Urinary alkalinization may help with salicylates.
Extracorporeal methods are costly and risky; use only if necessary.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.title: Indications for ICU Admission in Severe Poisoning
items.content: Patients with severe poisoning should be admitted to an intensive care unit if they exhibit coma, respiratory depression, hypotension, cardiac conduction abnormalities, arrhythmias, temperature dysregulation, seizures, or require close monitoring and specific interventions.
keyPoints: ICU admission is indicated for patients with severe poisoning showing: coma, respiratory depression, hypotension, cardiac conduction abnormalities, arrhythmias, hypothermia or hyperthermia, seizures
Patients needing close monitoring, antidotes, enhanced elimination therapy, those with progressive clinical deterioration, and significant underlying medical issues should be admitted to ICU
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 1)

items.title: Respiratory Care in Poisoning
items.content: Endotracheal intubation is crucial for patients with CNS depression or seizures to prevent aspiration. Mechanical ventilation may be necessary for respiratory depression, hypoxemia, and neuromuscular hyperactivity complications.
keyPoints: Endotracheal intubation is paramount in patients with CNS depression or seizures
Mechanical ventilation may be needed for respiratory depression, hypoxemia, or to prevent/treat hyperthermia, acidosis, rhabdomyolysis
Continuous pulse oximetry or arterial blood-gas analysis should determine the need for oxygenation and ventilation
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 1)

items.title: Cardiovascular Therapy in Poisoning
items.content: Maintaining normal tissue perfusion is critical. Use of norepinephrine, epinephrine, or high-dose dopamine may be necessary for unresponsive hypotension. Specific therapies are indicated for supraventricular tachycardia with hemodynamic instability.
keyPoints: Normal tissue perfusion maintenance is crucial for recovery
Norepinephrine, epinephrine, or high-dose dopamine may be needed if hypotension is unresponsive to volume expansion and antidotal therapy
Supraventricular tachycardia with hypertension and CNS excitation often requires benzodiazepines; severe cases may need labetalol, verapamil, diltiazem, or esmolol with nitroprusside
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 1)

items.title: Management of Ventricular Tachyarrhythmias in Toxicologic Context
items.content: For ventricular tachyarrhythmias suspected to be due to toxicologic causes, sodium bicarbonate is the first-line treatment. Class IA, IC, and III antiarrhythmic agents are contraindicated because they have similar electrophysiologic effects as the toxins. Intravenous lipid emulsion therapy can benefit arrhythmias from membrane-active agents. Magnesium sulfate and overdrive pacing may be useful for torsades de pointes with prolonged QT intervals.
keyPoints: Sodium bicarbonate is indicated for ventricular tachyarrhythmias due to tricyclic antidepressants and other membrane-active agents.
Class IA, IC, and III antiarrhythmic agents are contraindicated in these cases.
Intravenous lipid emulsion therapy can be beneficial for arrhythmias from membrane-active agents.
Magnesium sulfate and overdrive pacing may help with torsades de pointes and prolonged QT intervals.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 2)

items.title: Approach to Seizures in Toxicologic Emergencies
items.content: Seizures due to toxicologic causes should be treated aggressively. Benzodiazepines and barbiturates, which enhance GABA activity, are preferred for seizures from catecholamine receptor stimulation or decreased GABA/glycine activity. High doses of pyridoxine may be necessary for isoniazid-induced seizures. Phenytoin is contraindicated in toxicologic seizures due to worse outcomes. Dopamine receptor antagonists like haloperidol can manage psychotic behavior from dopaminergic poisons.
keyPoints: Benzodiazepines and barbiturates are preferred for treating seizures caused by catecholamine receptor stimulation or decreased GABA/glycine activity.
High doses of pyridoxine may be needed for isoniazid-induced seizures.
Phenytoin is contraindicated in toxicologic seizures due to worse outcomes.
Dopamine receptor antagonists can manage psychotic behavior from dopaminergic poisons.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 2)

items.title: Activated Charcoal as Preferred Gastrointestinal Decontamination
items.content: Activated charcoal is the preferred method for gastrointestinal decontamination due to its efficacy, fewer contraindications, and lower risk of complications compared to other methods like ipecac or gastric lavage.
keyPoints: Efficacy decreases with time; data insufficient >1 hour after ingestion.
Recommended dose is 1 g/kg body weight.
Adsorbs ≥90% of most substances when given in an amount equal to 10 times the weight of the substance.
Decreases absorption by 73% within 5 minutes, 51% at 30 minutes, and 36% at 60 minutes post-ingestion.
Not recommended for corrosives due to obscuring endoscopy.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 1)

items.title: Indications and Contraindications for Gastric Lavage
items.content: Gastric lavage is considered for life-threatening poisons not effectively treated by other means, but it has significant risks and contraindications.
keyPoints: Considered for life-threatening poisons like colchicine.
Decreases absorption by 52% within 5 minutes, 26% at 30 minutes, and 16% at 60 minutes post-ingestion.
Complications include aspiration (up to 10%) and serious complications like perforation (~1%).
Contraindicated in corrosive or petroleum distillate ingestions, compromised airways, hemorrhage risk, recent surgery, and non-cooperative patients.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 1)

items.title: Discontinuation of Syrup of Ipecac in Poison Management
items.content: Syrup of ipecac is no longer recommended for poisoning management due to its risks and lack of efficacy.
keyPoints: No role in current poisoning management; American Academy of Pediatrics recommends against use since 2003.
Chronic use can cause electrolyte abnormalities, cardiac toxicity, and myopathy.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 1)

items.title: Whole-bowel Irrigation: Indications and Contraindications
items.content: Whole-bowel irrigation is a procedure used to prevent the absorption of ingested poisons by administering a bowel-cleansing solution until rectal effluent is clear. It is particularly effective for substances poorly adsorbed by charcoal, such as heavy metals and certain drug packets.
keyPoints: Indicated for ingestion of foreign bodies, illicit drug packets, and agents poorly adsorbed by charcoal (e.g., heavy metals).
Contraindicated in patients with bowel obstruction, ileus, hemodynamic instability, and compromised unprotected airways.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 2)

items.title: Cathartics: Lack of Efficacy and Risks
items.content: Cathartics, historically used with activated charcoal to promote gastrointestinal evacuation, have no proven decontamination benefit. They can cause side effects like abdominal cramps and nausea, and repeated use may lead to severe electrolyte disturbances.
keyPoints: No evidence supports the decontamination benefit of cathartics in animal, volunteer, or clinical studies.
Contraindicated in patients with corrosive ingestion or preexisting diarrhea; magnesium-containing cathartics are contraindicated in renal failure.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 2)

items.title: Multiple-Dose Activated Charcoal for Poison Elimination
items.content: Therapeutic interventions like multiple-dose activated charcoal can accelerate poison elimination by binding substances in the gut. This method is effective for specific agents such as phenobarbital and theophylline, approaching the efficacy of hemodialysis.
keyPoints: Doses: 0.5–1 g/kg every 2–4 hours
Effective for: Theophylline, phenobarbital, carbamazepine, dapsone, quinine
Complications: Intestinal obstruction and nonocclusive intestinal infarction in patients with decreased gut motility
Cathartics contraindicated due to electrolyte shifts
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ENHANCEMENT OF POISON ELIMINATION

items.title: Urinary Alkalinization for Enhanced Excretion of Poisons
items.content: Altering urine pH through urinary alkalinization can enhance the excretion of certain poisons by ion trapping. This technique is particularly useful for acidic poisons, increasing their ionization and retention in alkaline urine.
keyPoints: Urine pH target: ≥7.5
Urine output: 3–6 mL/kg/hour with sodium bicarbonate IV solution
Effective for: Chlorophenoxyacetic acid herbicides, chlorpropamide, salicylates
Contraindications: Congestive heart failure, renal failure, cerebral edema
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ENHANCEMENT OF POISON ELIMINATION

items.title: Extracorporeal Removal Techniques for Severe Poisoning
items.content: Extracorporeal removal methods like hemodialysis and hemoperfusion are used to remove toxins from the bloodstream, especially in severe poisoning cases. These techniques are most effective for agents with specific pharmacokinetic properties.
keyPoints: Indications: Severe toxicity, prolonged or fatal toxicity, dangerous blood levels
Effective for: Ethylene glycol, lithium, methanol, salicylates
Considerations: Requires central venous access and systemic anticoagulation
Complications: Transient hypotension, hemolysis with hemoperfusion
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ENHANCEMENT OF POISON ELIMINATION

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE
items.title: Mechanisms and Indications for Antidote Administration
items.content: Antidotes work by neutralizing poisons through mechanisms such as antibody-antigen reactions, chelation, or chemical binding. They can also antagonize physiological effects by activating opposing nervous system activity or providing competitive metabolic or receptor substrates. Specific antidotes are available for various poisons including acetaminophen and opioids.
keyPoints: Antidotes neutralize poisons via antibody-antigen reactions, chelation, chemical binding.
They antagonize physiological effects by activating opposing nervous system activity or providing competitive substrates.
Specific antidotes exist for acetaminophen, anticholinergic agents, opioids, and more.
Intravenous lipid emulsion is effective for anesthetics and membrane-active agents but its mechanism is under investigation.
Antidotes reduce morbidity and mortality but can be toxic if misused.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE
items.title: Strategies for Preventing Reexposure to Poisons
items.content: Preventing poisoning involves educating patients on safe medication use, assisting confused individuals with medication administration, and notifying appropriate agencies in cases of environmental exposure. Limiting access to poisons is crucial for children and those at risk of intentional overdose.
keyPoints: Educate adults on safe medication and chemical use according to labeling instructions.
Assist confused patients with medication administration and address dosing errors by healthcare providers.
Notify agencies like OSHA in cases of environmental or workplace exposure.
Limit access to poisons for children and those at risk of intentional overdose.
Depressed, bipolar, or psychotic patients should undergo psychiatric assessment and be monitored for compliance.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Understanding the Evolving Risks of Poisoning Globally
items.content: The risks and patterns of poisoning are changing globally due to factors such as travel, immigration, internet consumerism, and climate change. These changes necessitate a broader approach in evaluating patients with suspected poisonings or overdoses.
keyPoints: Consider travel, immigration, and internet consumerism when assessing poisoning etiology.
Immigrants may have exposure to metals from previous environments.
Herbal remedies and imported products can be contaminated with toxic substances.
New drugs of abuse spread quickly via the internet.
Climate change affects geographic patterns of poisonings like snakebites.
Expanded history, global health specialist input, and broader lab panels may be needed.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.title: Impact of COVID-19 on Poisoning Patterns
items.content: The COVID-19 pandemic has led to an increase in poisonings from household cleaning substances and untested therapies, highlighting the need for vigilance during public health crises.
keyPoints: COVID-19 increased poisoning incidents from household cleaners.
Novel, untested therapies have contributed to poisoning cases.
Public health crises can alter poisoning patterns.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.question: Which group is most commonly involved in accidental poison exposures?
items.explanation: Most accidental poison exposures occur in children <6 years of age.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · Poisoning and Drug Overdose | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What percentage of poisoning exposures involve pharmaceuticals?
items.explanation: Pharmaceuticals are involved in 47% of poisoning exposures.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · Poisoning and Drug Overdose | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What is the leading cause of death from poisoning?
items.explanation: Carbon monoxide is the leading cause of death from poisoning.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · Poisoning and Drug Overdose | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: What percentage of all poison exposures result in hospitalization?
items.explanation: 5% of all poison exposures require hospitalization.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · Poisoning and Drug Overdose | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically included in the history when diagnosing poisoning?
options: Name and amount of each drug involved
items.explanation: The patient's favorite color is not relevant to diagnosing poisoning.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · DIAGNOSIS | HISTORY

items.question: What should be considered when a previously healthy person suddenly becomes ill without explanation?
options: Possible exposure to an unfamiliar poison
items.explanation: Unexplained sudden illness in a previously healthy person may suggest possible exposure to an unfamiliar poison.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · DIAGNOSIS | HISTORY

items.question: In cases where the patient is unable or unwilling to admit to exposure, what additional sources of information might be useful?
items.explanation: Information from family, friends, paramedics, police, pharmacists, physicians, and employers can be useful.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · DIAGNOSIS | HISTORY

items.question: Which of the following findings would most likely prompt evaluation for a structural central nervous system lesion in a poisoning case?
items.explanation: Focal neurologic findings are uncommon in poisoning and suggest a structural CNS lesion.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 1)

items.question: In cases of stimulant poisoning, which vital sign is the most reliable prognosticator of poor outcome?
items.explanation: Elevation in core temperature is the most reliable prognosticator of poor outcome in stimulant poisoning.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 1)

items.question: Which feature distinguishes anticholinergic toxidrome from other stimulant syndromes?
options: Hot, dry, flushed skin with urinary retention
items.explanation: Anticholinergic toxidrome is characterized by hot, dry, flushed skin and urinary retention.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 1)

items.question: What finding suggests a sympathetic etiology in poisoning?
items.explanation: Seizures suggest a sympathetic etiology, among other possibilities.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 1)

items.question: Which agents are most likely to cause miosis in the context of poisoning?
items.explanation: Miosis is most pronounced in opioid and cholinergic poisoning.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 2)

items.question: What condition might be suggested by the presence of mydriasis during a physiologic examination?
items.explanation: Mydriasis is associated with tricyclic antidepressants, some antiarrhythmics, meperidine, and diphenoxylate-atropine.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 2)

items.question: Which condition is characterized by simultaneous coma, seizures, hypotension, and tachyarrhythmias?
items.explanation: Membrane-active agents can cause simultaneous coma, seizures, hypotension, and tachyarrhythmias.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following conditions can cause an increased osmolal gap?
options: Acetone; alcohols such as ethanol and methanol
items.explanation: An increased osmolal gap suggests the presence of a low-molecular-weight solute such as acetone or alcohols like ethanol and methanol.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.question: Which laboratory finding is most commonly associated with advanced methanol intoxication?
items.explanation: Increased anion gap metabolic acidosis is most common in advanced methanol intoxication.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.question: Which condition can cause hypokalemia?
items.explanation: Hypokalemia can be caused by barium, among other substances.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.question: What is a potential cause of pulmonary edema in poisoning cases?
options: Bradycardia due to calcium channel blockers
items.explanation: Pulmonary edema can be caused by carbon monoxide poisoning.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a limitation of rapid qualitative hospital-based urine tests for drugs of abuse?
options: They are confirmatory and can be used for forensic purposes.
False-positive results are uncommon with these tests.
They cannot confirm the exact identity of the detected substance.
Results from these tests are available within an hour.
items.explanation: Rapid qualitative urine tests are only screening tests and cannot confirm the exact identity of substances.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.question: In which scenario is it most clinically useful to perform a qualitative drug screen?
options: When patients present with mild symptoms that are consistent with their history.
For asymptomatic patients when the clinical picture matches reported history.
For severe or unexplained toxicities such as coma and seizures.
In cases of suspected poisoning by synthetic cannabinoids.
items.explanation: Qualitative drug screens are most valuable for evaluating patients with severe or unexplained toxicities.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following substances is NOT typically detectable by hospital-based rapid qualitative urine tests?
items.explanation: Synthetic cannabinoids are not detectable by hospital-based rapid qualitative urine tests.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.question: Which phase of poisoning is characterized by the highest priority for decontamination based solely on history?
items.explanation: Decontamination is prioritized in the pretoxic phase, where treatment is based on history before symptoms appear.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.question: When should blood and urine be sent for toxicologic screening if a poison causing delayed toxicity or irreversible damage is suspected?
options: Only after the patient becomes symptomatic
If an accurate history cannot be obtained
items.explanation: Blood and urine should be sent for toxicologic screening if a poison causing delayed toxicity is suspected and an accurate history is not obtainable.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.question: What is the general guideline for discharging patients who have ingested poisons?
options: Discharge after 6 hours if they remain asymptomatic
Only discharge after complete resolution of symptoms
Discharge immediately if no history of ingestion is available
Never discharge without a toxicologic screening
items.explanation: Patients who remain asymptomatic or become so within 6 hours are unlikely to develop toxicity and can be discharged safely.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.question: Which intervention is less likely to be effective during the toxic phase compared to the pretoxic phase?
items.explanation: Decontamination is less likely to be effective during the toxic phase than during the pretoxic phase.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.question: Which of the following patients should be admitted to an intensive care unit for severe poisoning?
options: Patients with mild to moderate toxicity who can be managed on a general medical service.
Patients experiencing coma, respiratory depression, or hypotension.
Patients showing no signs of clinical deterioration and have no significant underlying medical problems.
Patients who attempted suicide but are no longer considered at risk.
items.explanation: Intensive care is required for patients with severe poisoning symptoms such as coma, respiratory depression, or hypotension.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 1)

items.question: What is the primary reason for endotracheal intubation in patients with CNS depression?
options: To protect against aspiration of gastrointestinal contents.
items.explanation: Endotracheal intubation is crucial for preventing the aspiration of gastrointestinal contents in patients with CNS depression or seizures.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 1)

items.question: Which therapy should be used according to protocol after a return of spontaneous circulation following resuscitation from poisoning-induced cardiopulmonary arrest?
items.explanation: Therapeutic hypothermia is recommended for patients who regain spontaneous circulation after resuscitation from poisoning-induced cardiopulmonary arrest.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following treatments may be effective in beta blocker and calcium channel blocker poisoning?
options: Glucagon, calcium, and high-dose insulin with dextrose.
items.explanation: In beta blocker and calcium channel blocker poisoning, glucagon, calcium, and high-dose insulin with dextrose can be effective treatments.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 1)

items.question: Which agent is contraindicated for ventricular tachyarrhythmias due to tricyclic antidepressants?
options: Class IA, IC, and III antiarrhythmic agents
items.explanation: Class IA, IC, and III antiarrhythmic agents are contraindicated due to similar electrophysiologic effects.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 2)

items.question: Which therapy has shown benefit for arrhythmias from various membrane-active agents?
items.explanation: Intravenous lipid emulsion therapy has shown benefit for treatment of arrhythmias from various membrane-active agents.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 2)

items.question: What is the first-line treatment for seizures caused by excessive stimulation of catecholamine receptors?
items.explanation: Seizures caused by excessive stimulation of catecholamine receptors are best treated with agents that enhance GABA activity, such as benzodiazepines or barbiturates.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a contraindication for using activated charcoal in gastrointestinal decontamination?
items.explanation: Activated charcoal is not effective for charged (ionized) chemicals such as highly dissociated salts.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended dose of activated charcoal for gastrointestinal decontamination?
options: 10 times the weight of the substance
items.explanation: The generally recommended dose is 1 g/kg body weight.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 1)

items.question: Which position should a patient be placed in during gastric lavage to prevent aspiration?
items.explanation: The patient should be placed in Trendelenburg and left lateral decubitus positions.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 1)

items.question: What is the average decrease in ingestant absorption when activated charcoal is given within 5 minutes of ingestion?
items.explanation: Charcoal decreases the absorption by an average of 73% when given within 5 min.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a contraindication for whole-bowel irrigation?
items.explanation: Whole-bowel irrigation is contraindicated in patients with bowel obstruction.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 2)

items.question: For which of the following ingestions is cathartics most appropriately avoided?
items.explanation: Cathartics are contraindicated in patients who have ingested corrosives.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended initial treatment for topical exposures to poisons?
options: Copious flushing with water, saline, or another clear liquid
items.explanation: The initial treatment for topical exposures is immediate, copious flushing with water, saline, or another available clear, drinkable liquid.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 2)

items.question: Which agents are considered for multiple-dose activated charcoal therapy?
items.explanation: Multiple-dose activated charcoal therapy is considered for theophylline, phenobarbital, and carbamazepine.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ENHANCEMENT OF POISON ELIMINATION

items.question: What is a contraindication for multiple doses of activated charcoal?
items.explanation: Complications such as intestinal obstruction and pseudo-obstruction can occur in patients with decreased gut motility, making it a contraindication.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ENHANCEMENT OF POISON ELIMINATION

items.question: Which condition is NOT an indication for urinary alkalinization?
items.explanation: Urinary alkalinization is contraindicated in congestive heart failure.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ENHANCEMENT OF POISON ELIMINATION

items.question: Which characteristic makes a toxin amenable to hemodialysis?
items.explanation: Toxins with prolonged elimination and low volume of distribution are amenable to hemodialysis.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ENHANCEMENT OF POISON ELIMINATION

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE
items.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a poison or condition with a specific antidote in the passage?
items.explanation: Diabetes mellitus is not mentioned as having a specific antidote in the passage.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE
items.question: What is one potential risk associated with the use of antidotes?
options: They are only effective in children.
They can be potentially toxic if used inappropriately.
items.explanation: Antidotes can be potentially toxic if used for inappropriate reasons, as stated in the passage.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE
items.question: What is a common cause of unintentional polypharmacy poisoning among adults?
options: Developmental delays and large number of prescribed medications.
items.explanation: Unintentional polypharmacy poisoning is common among adults with developmental delays and those prescribed a large number of medications, as mentioned in the passage.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: Which chapter discusses the neuroleptic malignant syndrome?
items.explanation: The neuroleptic malignant syndrome is discussed in Chap. 446.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: Which factor has been identified as a reason for evolving geographic patterns of poisoning injuries from snakebites?
items.explanation: Climate change is identified as a key reason for evolving differences in the geographic pattern of poisoning injuries from snakebites.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.question: What should be considered when evaluating patients suspected of poisoning without clear etiology?
items.explanation: Patterns of travel, immigration, and internet consumerism should always be considered in such cases.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.statement: Fatalities from drug overdose in the United States have decreased since 1999.
items.explanation: Fatalities involving drugs have steadily increased since 1999.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · Poisoning and Drug Overdose | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Acetaminophen is the pharmaceutical agent most often implicated in fatal poisoning.
items.explanation: Acetaminophen is indeed the pharmaceutical agent most often implicated in fatal poisoning.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · Poisoning and Drug Overdose | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: A search of the patient's belongings may reveal a suicide note or container of drugs.
items.explanation: Searching clothes and belongings can uncover important clues like a suicide note or drug containers.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · DIAGNOSIS | HISTORY

items.statement: The patient's favorite color is crucial for diagnosing poisoning.
items.explanation: The patient's favorite color is not relevant to the diagnosis of poisoning.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · DIAGNOSIS | HISTORY

items.statement: Focal neurologic findings are common in poisoning cases.
items.explanation: Focal neurologic findings are uncommon and suggest a structural CNS lesion.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 1)

items.statement: Increased core temperature is not an important prognosticator in stimulant poisoning.
items.explanation: Core temperature elevation is the most reliable prognosticator of poor outcome in stimulant poisoning.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 1)

items.statement: Cardiac arrhythmias in poisoning are often due to sedative-hypnotic agents.
items.explanation: Cardiac arrhythmias and conduction disturbances are due to antiarrhythmics, β-adrenergic antagonists, calcium channel blockers, digitalis glycosides, propoxyphene, and cyclic antidepressants.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 2)

items.statement: A normal physiologic state can be due to a toxic time-bomb exposure.
items.explanation: Toxic time-bombs are agents that are slowly absorbed or metabolized, which may result in a normal physiologic state initially.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 2)

items.statement: Prothrombin time and international normalized ratio are reliable for evaluating overdose complications from direct oral anticoagulants.
items.explanation: These tests should not be relied on when evaluating overdose or complications from direct oral anticoagulant pharmaceuticals.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.statement: An increased anion gap can occur with any poisoning that results in hepatic, renal, or respiratory failure.
items.explanation: Increased anion gap is common in poisonings leading to such failures.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.statement: Confirmatory testing with gas chromatography/mass spectrometry is typically available within a few days.
items.explanation: Confirmatory testing often takes weeks to obtain results.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.statement: Quantitative serum tests are useful for evaluating patients poisoned with substances like acetaminophen and lithium.
items.explanation: Quantitative serum tests guide clinical management for these poisons.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 2)

items.statement: Blood levels of a poison are always indicative of its toxicity.
items.explanation: Blood levels may not correlate with toxicity, especially if metabolites are more toxic than the parent compound.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.statement: Patients who remain asymptomatic for 6 hours after ingestion can generally be discharged safely.
items.explanation: Most patients who remain asymptomatic or become so within 6 hours are unlikely to develop toxicity and can be discharged.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.statement: The gag reflex is a reliable indicator of the need for intubation in patients with CNS depression.
items.explanation: The gag reflex is not a reliable indicator; clinical assessment can be inaccurate, and continuous monitoring is recommended.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 1)

items.statement: Drug-induced pulmonary edema is usually of cardiac origin.
items.explanation: Drug-induced pulmonary edema is typically noncardiac in origin, though profound CNS depression and cardiac abnormalities suggest a cardiac cause.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 1)

items.statement: Phenytoin is recommended for toxicologic seizures.
items.explanation: Phenytoin is contraindicated in toxicologic seizures as it leads to worse outcomes.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 2)

items.statement: Magnesium sulfate may be useful for torsades des pointes and prolonged QT intervals.
items.explanation: Magnesium sulfate is mentioned as potentially useful in patients with torsades des pointes and prolonged QT intervals.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 2)

items.statement: Gastric lavage is recommended for all patients with suspected poisoning.
items.explanation: Gastric lavage should be considered selectively, not routinely.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 1)

items.statement: Ipecac syrup is recommended by the American Academy of Pediatrics for poisoning treatment.
items.explanation: The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends against using ipecac in poisoning treatment.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 1)

items.statement: Whole-bowel irrigation should be used for patients who have ingested agents poorly adsorbed by charcoal.
items.explanation: Whole-bowel irrigation is appropriate for ingestion of agents that are poorly adsorbed by charcoal.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 2)

items.statement: Dilution with water or another clear liquid is recommended after ingestion of non-corrosive substances.
items.explanation: Dilution is recommended only after the ingestion of corrosives, not non-corrosive substances.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 2)

items.statement: Multiple-dose activated charcoal can be used safely in patients with decreased gastrointestinal motility.
items.explanation: Decreased gastrointestinal motility is a contraindication for multiple doses of activated charcoal due to risks like intestinal obstruction.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ENHANCEMENT OF POISON ELIMINATION

items.statement: Hemoperfusion can correct associated acid-base and electrolyte abnormalities.
items.explanation: Hemoperfusion does not correct acid-base and electrolyte abnormalities, which is a limitation compared to hemodialysis.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ENHANCEMENT OF POISON ELIMINATION

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE
items.statement: Intravenous lipid emulsion is an established antidote with a well-understood mechanism for various poisonings.
items.explanation: The exact mechanism of benefit from intravenous lipid emulsion is still under investigation.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE

items.subtopic: ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE
items.statement: Expanding naloxone distribution should be a priority in regions with high incidence of opioid overdose.
items.explanation: The passage states that expanding naloxone distribution is a priority in such regions.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ADMINISTRATION OF ANTIDOTES | PREVENTION OF REEXPOSURE

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.statement: Herbal remedies imported from overseas may be contaminated with toxic plants.
items.explanation: The passage states that herbal remedies, food products, and cosmetics imported from overseas or ordered from the internet may be contaminated.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS
items.statement: Acetaminophen poisoning is discussed in Chap. 467.
items.explanation: Acetaminophen poisoning is actually discussed in Chap. 351.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SPECIFIC TOXIC SYNDROMES AND POISONINGS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.assertion: The majority of serious poisonings are due to intentional self-harm.
items.reason: Fatalities most commonly occur from intentional self-harm ingestions (suicide) of pharmaceuticals or from complications involving opioids and other psychoactive drugs of abuse.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · Poisoning and Drug Overdose | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Most poison exposures result in serious toxicity.
items.reason: More than 5 million poison exposures occur annually in the United States, most of which are accidental and involve minor or no toxicity.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; most poison exposures do not result in serious toxicity.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · Poisoning and Drug Overdose | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.assertion: Mydriasis is a characteristic feature of all stimulants.
items.reason: Pupillary reactivity relies on muscarinic control, which is blocked in anticholinergic poisoning.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason correctly explains why mydriasis is most marked in anticholinergic poisoning.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 1)

items.reason: Seizures can indicate sympathetic etiology or membrane-active anticholinergic agents.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for seizures suggesting a withdrawal syndrome.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION AND CLINICAL COURSE (part 1)

items.assertion: Hypokalemia can be caused by diuretics.
items.reason: Diuretics increase renal potassium excretion, leading to hypokalemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.assertion: An abnormally low anion gap can be due to elevated blood levels of lithium.
items.reason: Lithium is a cation that contributes to an increased, not decreased, anion gap.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because lithium causes an increased anion gap.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · LABORATORY ASSESSMENT (part 1)

items.assertion: Enhancing poison elimination may shorten the duration of the toxic phase.
items.reason: These measures carry risks which must be balanced against potential benefits.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why enhancing elimination shortens the toxic phase.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.assertion: During the resolution phase, blood levels of a poison always correlate with toxicity.
items.reason: Chemicals are eliminated from blood sooner than tissues during this phase.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because blood levels may not correlate with toxicity due to earlier elimination from blood.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · GENERAL PRINCIPLES

items.assertion: Supraventricular tachycardia with hypertension is often due to agents causing generalized physiologic excitation.
items.reason: This type of tachycardia generally requires only observation or nonspecific sedation with a benzodiazepine.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why mild to moderate cases require minimal intervention.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 1)

items.assertion: In patients with supraventricular tachycardia without hypertension, treatment typically involves high-dose benzodiazepines.
items.reason: This condition is generally secondary to vasodilation or hypovolemia and responds well to fluid administration.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the correct treatment for supraventricular tachycardia without hypertension involves fluid administration, not high-dose benzodiazepines.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · SUPPORTIVE CARE (part 1)

items.assertion: Activated charcoal is the preferred method for gastrointestinal decontamination.
items.reason: It has fewer contraindications and complications compared to other methods.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 1)

items.assertion: Gastric lavage is contraindicated in patients with a compromised unprotected airway.
items.reason: It can lead to aspiration pneumonitis.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; it's contraindicated due to risk of aspiration, not specifically pneumonitis.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · PREVENTION OF POISON ABSORPTION (part 1)

items.assertion: Urinary alkalinization enhances the excretion of salicylates.
items.reason: Alkaline urine prevents the reabsorption of ionized acidic poisons, trapping them in the renal tubules.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why urinary alkalinization enhances salicylate excretion.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ENHANCEMENT OF POISON ELIMINATION

items.assertion: Extracorporeal removal is only indicated for patients with mild toxicity.
items.reason: These therapies are invasive and carry risks such as hypotension and thrombocytopenia.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; extracorporeal removal is indicated in severe toxicity, not mild.
items.reference: Poisoning and Drug Overdose · ENHANCEMENT OF POISON ELIMINATION
